planting.note: Root softwood cuttings under mist in early summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; star magnolia is one of the few magnolias that tolerates partial shade and maintains good flower production — but flowers may be damaged by late spring frosts, which is one reason for siting it in a position with some north or east exposure to delay bloom until frost risk has passed.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; star magnolia is a Japanese deciduous shrub-tree that flowers earlier than virtually any other ornamental tree in the temperate garden — in late March to April (zone 5–7), or even late February to early March in milder zones, the still-bare, furry-budded branches burst open into masses of large, multi-petaled, pure-white (or pale-pink in 'Rosea'), star-shaped flowers with 12–18 narrow, strap-like petals that radiate in all directions from the center. The pure-white, star-like flowers cover every branch and create an extraordinary white cloud effect before a single leaf has emerged. It blooms 2–3 weeks before Magnolia x soulangeana and considerably earlier than most garden shrubs, making it the first dramatic flowering statement of the temperate garden year. The narrow petals give it a distinctive, finely structured appearance quite different from the cupped, tulip-flowered magnolias. It is smaller and more shrubby in habit than many magnolias. 'Royal Star' (late-flowering, avoiding late frosts, particularly large flowers), 'Waterlily' (pink-budded, white-opening, very fragrant), and 'Centennial' are excellent cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings under mist in early summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; Loebner magnolia blooms 1–2 weeks later than Magnolia stellata, making it slightly less susceptible to late-frost flower damage — an important practical advantage in zone 4–5 gardens.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; Loebner magnolia is a hybrid between Magnolia kobus (Kobus magnolia) and M. stellata (star magnolia), combining the tree-like stature and vigor of the kobus parent with the larger, more numerous flower petals and earlier flowering of the stellata parent to produce a superior garden tree. In April the bare branches become covered with large, many-petaled, white to pale-pink, starry, fragrant flowers before the leaves emerge, creating a spectacular spring display. The cultivar 'Merrill' — vigorous, reliably flowering at a young age, and very cold-hardy — is the most widely planted and commercially available selection, and is considered one of the finest magnolias for zones 4–8. 'Leonard Messel' produces pale-pink to rose-purple flowers with typically 12 petals and is outstanding in a soft-pink color scheme. Both are superior to M. stellata in size, vigor, and visual impact. Tolerates partial shade and clay soil better than most magnolias. An excellent choice as a single specimen or small grove in lawns, borders, and naturalistic gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in midsummer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; Magnolia sieboldii is one of the most shade-tolerant magnolia species and blooms more reliably in partial-shade positions than virtually any other magnolia — siting in partial shade also slows spring growth and reduces the risk of early flowers being caught by late frosts.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Oyama magnolia is an East Asian deciduous magnolia that flowers in late spring to early summer (May–June) — well after frost risk has passed — producing pendant, nodding, cup-shaped, pure-white, intensely lemon-and-vanilla-fragrant flowers with a prominent central boss of deep crimson-red stamens that contrast dramatically with the white petals and give each flower a jewel-like center. The flowers nod downward on their stems and are best viewed from below or on a gentle slope, where their pendant display and scarlet centers are most visible. It is one of the very few magnolias that performs in genuine partial shade, blooms reliably in dappled light, and does not require full sun for good flowering — making it uniquely valuable for shaded woodland gardens where other magnolias would fail to bloom properly. The large, bold, bright-green leaves are themselves attractive. A refined, fragrant, and very different-looking magnolia suited to the naturalistic shade garden rather than the formal sunny border.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in midsummer under mist or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; Wilson's magnolia is best appreciated when planted at the top of a slope, retaining wall, or above a path so that the pendulous, nodding flowers can be admired at face level or viewed from below.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Wilson's magnolia is a Chinese deciduous magnolia introduced from Sichuan province by plant hunter E.H. Wilson in 1908 that produces, in May and June, the most exquisitely formed pendant flowers of any magnolia species — pure-white, circular, saucer-shaped, deeply cupped flowers about 3–4 inches across, with nine large, rounded, pure-white petals surrounding a magnificent, bold, central boss of deep crimson-red to rose-red stamens, hanging nodding on slender stalks from the tips of the branches so that they look directly downward, like ornamental lanterns. The brilliant contrast of the white petals against the vivid crimson stamen cone makes each flower a miniature work of art. Like M. sieboldii, it performs well in partial shade — making it particularly valuable for the woodland garden — and the late flowering season (May–June) means frost damage to the flowers is rare. A connoisseur's magnolia for zone 6–9 gardens seeking unusual shade-tolerant flowering trees. Closely related to and similar to M. sieboldii, distinguished by its slightly larger flowers and different leaf shape.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in midsummer under mist or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; banana shrub is slow-growing but extremely long-lived and low-maintenance once established — it should not be heavily pruned as this removes the flowering wood and reduces the fragrant display.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; banana shrub (formerly Michelia figo, now reclassified as Magnolia figo) is a Chinese evergreen magnolia relative that has been a classic southern garden shrub for over a century — prized not for showy flowers but for the intensely, exotically sweet, banana-custard-like fragrance of its small, creamy-yellow, purple-edged, tulip-shaped flowers that are produced in abundance from spring through fall amid the dense, glossy, dark-green, leathery leaves. The flowers are small but individually so powerfully fragrant — a single established shrub can perfume an entire garden — that they are worth seeking out close up, where the heavy, tropical sweetness creates an extraordinary sensory experience disproportionate to the flower's size. Banana shrub grows as a dense, compact, slow-growing, rounded evergreen shrub with a formal, refined habit and requires minimal care. It tolerates partial shade well and is one of the most versatile and dependable fragrant shrubs for zone 7–11, performing reliably for decades with little attention. 'Port Wine' (deep-burgundy purple flowers with stronger fragrance) is an outstanding cultivar.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
